reasoning: eter appears in 'eter ekuer' which corresponds to 'ate and drank' in the translation
ḫattešar is the base form of ḫattešnaš which appears in the context 'from the hole'
ûit appears with singular subjects: 'ḫupašiyašša ûit' (Hupasiyas came) and 'imaš ûit' (Stormgod came)
illuyankaš/illuyankan appears throughout the text referring to the snake
našta appears at the beginning of sentences corresponding to 'And' in the translation
šarā appears in 'šarā kallišta' and 'šarā ûēr' corresponding to 'up' in 'called up' and 'came up'
ekuer appears multiple times where 'drank' appears in the translation, with plural past tense marking
–aš appears in ḫattešnaš and illuyankaš marking the subject/nominative case
–ša appears as an enclitic marker that can indicate conjunction, seen in ḫupašiyašša
–meš clearly marks plural in dumumeššu (sons) and dingirmešša (gods)
–er/ēr appears in verbs with plural subjects: ûēr, eter, ekuer, ešer indicating 3rd person plural past tense
–an appears in illuyankan when the snake is the direct object being acted upon
–anna appears in adanna akuwanna corresponding to infinitives 'to eat and to drink'
–it appears in ûit with singular subjects (Hupasiyas, Stormgod) indicating 3rd person singular past tense
